summary: DHH announces the launch of HEY Calendar, the most-requested feature for their email service HEY.com. The calendar was designed from scratch rather than copying existing grid-based designs, drawing inspiration from paper family calendars. Originally planned as a separate paid product, 37signals decided to bundle it free with the existing HEY subscription at no price increase, betting that removing the calendar objection will push fence-sitters to switch from Gmail or Outlook.
key_points: The lack of an integrated calendar was the single biggest blocker preventing potential customers from switching to HEY
Rather than shipping a checkbox feature, they spent a full year rethinking calendar design from first principles, inspired by paper calendars
They pivoted from a separate paid product to bundling it free with HEY, making it a more compelling all-in-one package
The strategy explicitly targets user inertia — people stuck on Gmail/Outlook need serious force to change habits
money_quotes: If we were going to do a calendar for HEY, it had to be as interesting, inspiring, and innovative as HEY itself.
What should a calendar look like and how should it work if you designed it from a clean sheet of paper?
Which lead us to break the boring grid of a typical computer calendar, and add back a lot of that personality and whimsy that's possible with paper.
Inertia runs most people's lives. If you want to change that trajectory, you better apply some serious force.
We didn't just want to tick a box on a feature chart.
key_insight: Bundling a rethought calendar free with HEY is a deliberate strategy to overcome the inertia that keeps people locked into Gmail and Outlook.
